coredata.dc:title: A unique “turn-on” fluorescence signalling strategy for highly specific detection of ascorbic acid using carbon dots as sensing probe
coredata.dc:creator: Fong, Jessica Fung Yee
Chin, Suk Fun
Ng, Sing Muk
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) that showed strong blue fluorescence were successfully synthesised from sodium alginate via furnace pyrolysis. The single step pyrolytic synthesis was simple to perform while yielded CDs with high photostability, good water solubility and minimum by-products. In order to design the probe with “turn-on” sensing capability, the CDs were screened against a series of metal cations to first “turn-off” the fluorescence. It was found that ferric ions (Fe3+) were most responsive and effective in quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this observation, the conditioning of the probe was performed to ensure the fluorescence was completely quenched, while not overloading the system with Fe3+. At the optimised condition, the CDs-Fe3+ mixture served as a highly specific detection probe for ascorbic acid (AA). The analytical potential of the probe was evaluated and showed a good linear range of response for AA concentration of 24–40μg/mL. The selectivity study against other possible co-existing species was carried out and proved that our unique “turn-on” fluorescence signalling strategy was highly effective and selective towards AA as the target analyte. The probe was demonstrated for quantification of AA in real samples, which was the commercially available vitamin C supplement. The result showed good accuracy with minimum deviation from standard method adopted for validation purpose.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence “turn-on”
Carbon dots
Sustainable sensing
Optical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Probing and optimisation of sensing system
Conditioning and probing of sensing system
Effect of pH on CDs-Fe3+ sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by Swinburne Sarawak Research Grant phase 1/2013 (2-5509) from Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus . Jessica Fong would like to thank Swinburne SUPRA Scholarship for supporting her PhD study. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs; and (B) Size distribution of CDs based on the TEM analysis.

Photoluminescence profile of the CDs: absorbance spectrum (dotted lines), excitation spectrum when emission was set at 450nm (smooth left line) and the smooth right line represents the fluorescence emission of CDs upon excitation at 350nm.

(A) The fluorescence changes of CDs after addition of a series of metal cations. The order of metal ions was arranged by ascending oxidation states from left to right. The most right one added with water as control set. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of CDs with increasing amount of Fe3+ added; and (C) the changes of fluorescence intensity at various concentrations of Fe3+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Fe3+ complex which has been recovered after micro-titration of AA (from 0 to 66μg/mL); (B) F2/F1 plotted against the concentration of AA and the F2/F1 versus concentration of AA plot in the linear dynamic range (inset); and (C) The changes of fluorescence intensities of CDs-Fe complex in the presence of AA and other possible interfering species.

The effect of pH on the (A) fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ to form CDs-Fe3+ sensing system and (B) fluorescence restoration of CDs-Fe3+ by the addition of AA that can be utilised for sensing application.
AA detection in vitamin C tablet.

row.entry: Amount of AA recorded using our system (mg)
Amount of AA recorded using DCPIP method (mg)

head.ce:title: A unique “turn-on” fluorescence signalling strategy for highly specific detection of ascorbic acid using carbon dots as sensing probe

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) that showed strong blue fluorescence were successfully synthesised from sodium alginate via furnace pyrolysis. The single step pyrolytic synthesis was simple to perform while yielded CDs with high photostability, good water solubility and minimum by-products. In order to design the probe with “turn-on” sensing capability, the CDs were screened against a series of metal cations to first “turn-off” the fluorescence. It was found that ferric ions (Fe3+) were most responsive and effective in quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this observation, the conditioning of the probe was performed to ensure the fluorescence was completely quenched, while not overloading the system with Fe3+. At the optimised condition, the CDs-Fe3+ mixture served as a highly specific detection probe for ascorbic acid (AA). The analytical potential of the probe was evaluated and showed a good linear range of response for AA concentration of 24–40μg/mL. The selectivity study against other possible co-existing species was carried out and proved that our unique “turn-on” fluorescence signalling strategy was highly effective and selective towards AA as the target analyte. The probe was demonstrated for quantification of AA in real samples, which was the commercially available vitamin C supplement. The result showed good accuracy with minimum deviation from standard method adopted for validation purpose.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) or more well-known as vitamin C by the community is a strong antioxidant that can reduce oxidative stress in body and therefore believed to be able to lower cancer and cardiovascular risks (Alpsoy and Yalvac, 2011; Bendich et al., 1986). In addition, AA is essentially taken in our daily diet to fulfil the body requirement for the development and maintenance of scar tissue, blood vessels and cartilage. Besides, AA is involved directly in creating a series of other small biomolecules such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP), dopamine, peptide hormones, etc. (Naidu, 2003). The deficiency of AA in the body will lead to a medical condition termed as scurvy (Magiorkinis et al., 2011). Scurvy patients are incapable of generating collagen that is vital for new tissue replacement (Agarwal et al., 2015). An excessive amount of AA may not be harmful to us but an overdose of AA from supplements may result in conditions such as diarrhoea, nausea, heartburn, kidney stones, etc. As recommended by Food and Nutrition Board under the Institute of Medicine of the National Academies, the adequate daily intake of vitamin C for a male adult is 90mg by average and 75mg for a female adult. The tolerable limit for daily intake is estimated at 2000mg per day for male and female adults (I.o.M.F.a.N. and Board, 2001). Considering the important roles of AA in our daily life, it will be beneficial to monitor and determine its content in food and pharmaceutical products to ensure adequate intake. Besides, AA detection in blood serum is also an important diagnosis of scurvy.

There are plenty of methods dedicated for the detection of AA. Despite this, there are still continuous efforts in the search for better and more specific detection of AA for diagnosis purposes and daily uses for the public. AA is readily available in our daily life, whether in fresh vegetables and fruits or in the form of pharmaceutical products such as vitamin C or multivitamin tablets. It would be handy to have a portable sensor for AA to be used in our daily life. Otherwise, the existing methods are mainly carried out in the laboratory, such as colorimetric methods, titrimetry, voltammetry, potentiometry, chromatography, flow injection analyses, and etc. which are not very accessible to the public for fast and efficient detection of AA (Arya et al., 2000). These listed methods are mostly time-consuming and involve costly and complicated instrumental setups.

Optical sensing using fluorescence has been a popular option for many applications due to the high sensitivity and low limit of detection. The principle commonly employed is the basic fluorescence quenching where the decrease in emission intensity after the addition of analyte is taken as the sensing signal. However in some cases, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered using competitive ligands, which is also termed fluorescence turn “off and on”. This can be adopted as a good sensing mechanism where a quencher that effectively reduces the emission intensity is first added to the system and later can be recovered quantitatively by the analyte of interest. The analyte added should have a strong binding affinity towards the quencher to allow disruption of the initial short-range interaction between the fluorophore and quencher. Subsequently, the fluorophore will be freed from the quencher. This leads to the restoration of fluorescence which allows efficient detection of the target analyte. The extra step for this technique could lower the possibilities of false positive due to environmental stimulus in the single-step “turn-off” models (Liu et al., 2015). Ideally, fluorescence based optical sensing system requires a high-quality fluorophore which fluoresces strongly, resistant towards photobleaching and able to be quenched effectively by a specific quencher.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a newly found class of carbon nanomaterials that are recently discovered in a serendipitous event of electrophoresis purification of synthesised single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). A large fluorescent fast moving band was observed as a new species other than the SWCNTs (Xu et al., 2004), which was later discovered as CDs. These CDs are then labelled as an alternative to quantum dots as bio-sensing and bio-imaging agent due to their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity which minimise the cytotoxicity and body clearance issues of quantum dots (Baker and Baker, 2010). Amongst the sensing applications based on CDs, most of them are established by observation of direct emission quenching as sensing signal (Qu et al., 2012; Song et al., 2014) The fluorescence “turn-on” models based on CDs are not very common and widely explored yet. Although there are some studies working on “turn-off-on” sensing (He et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2015a, 2015b), this area definitely has great potential to be explored further. These CDs could be obtained in two synthetic routes, namely the bottom-up and top-down approaches. The top-down approach involves physical treatment such as laser ablation (Sun et al., 2006), arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004), electrochemical discharge (Ming et al., 2012), etc. to form CDs by breaking off the larger carbon structures into smaller clusters (Baker and Baker, 2010). This approach requires more complicated instrumental setups and costly starting materials such as graphene, etc. To resolve this issue, most researchers have shifted focus to the bottom-up approach which is less complex while also greener in the synthesis route. The bottom-up route is the chemical method where the molecular precursors build up nanoparticles in a controlled manner (Baker and Baker, 2010). A few examples of bottom-up approach include the microwave method (Zhu et al., 2009), combustion/thermal (Liu et al., 2007; Tan et al., 2014; Tian et al., 2009), acid dehydration (Fong et al., 2015; Wee et al., 2013) and etc.

In this work, we report a simple pyrolytic synthesis of the blue emitting CDs from commercially available sodium alginate, extracted from brown algae. Our synthetic approach has successfully demonstrated a better control in the emission of CDs by monitoring the temperature during the pyrolytic synthesis. The CDs collected were paired with an effective quencher, ferric ion (Fe3+), to “turn-off” the fluorescence. This later suited the fluorescence “turn-on” model that allowed the determination of AA, which is the targeted analyte of interest for this study. This method allowed the determination of a well-known antioxidant, AA due to their chelating nature towards Fe3+, disrupting the loose interaction with the CDs and hence regaining the fluorescence of CDs. Although another group by Gong has worked on the similar concept on AA sensing, it is worth noting that the starting precursor, the synthesis pathway and the optical properties of CDs obtained are completely different (Gong et al., 2015). This has diversified the insight of using a straightforward and simple method to produce CDs from cheap natural product. The starting material, sodium alginate, used in this study is commonly used in food preparation and the low pricing will render it cost effective, especially for potential sensor development such as ‘single-spot’ analysis.

Sodium alginate, L-ascorbic acid, arginine, thiamine, L-glutamic acid and L-cysteine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium nitrate (KNO3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), chromium nitrate (Cr(NO3)3), zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2), iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3), mercury chloride (HgCl2), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), tin chloride (SnCl2), glucose, glycine, calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) and meta-phosphoric acid were purchased from R&M Marketing (Malaysia). Glacial acetic acid was purchased from JT Baker (UK) and 2,6-dichlorophenolindophenol (DCPIP) was obtained from UniChem (Malaysia). All the chemicals were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout the experiments was obtained from Milli-Q millipore system (18.2MΩ).

All the pyrolysis steps were carried out in a laboratory furnace (Carbolite ELF 11/14 B) with a maximum setting temperature of 1100°C. Freeze drying of CDs product was carried out in benchtop freeze dryer (Labconco) at −50°C and 0.05atm pressure. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, with optimised settings of 10nm for both excitation and emission slits. UV–vis spectra were obtained from Varian Cary© 50 Conc spectrophotometer, dual beam settings. All spectra were recorded in a quartz cuvette with a pathlength of 1.0cm. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from PerkinElmer Nicolet iS10. Dispersed CDs samples in ultrapure water were dropped onto formvar-coated copper grids and observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL Model JEM 1010).

1.0g of sodium alginate was weighed into a crucible and heated for 1h in a furnace. Different carbonisation temperatures of 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350°C were tested for optimisation purposes. The crucible containing the pyrolysed alginate, observed as dark brown residues were removed from the furnace with extra care and left to cool down to room temperature. To remove the larger particles, water was added to dissolve the crude CDs, followed by filtration through 0.22µm PTFE membrane filter with a diameter of 25mm. Then, the water was removed via freeze-drying overnight. The dried CDs product was kept in a desiccator until further use. For the following analysis use, 2.0mg/mL of CDs were prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing and optimisation of sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: 50µL of CDs (2.0mg/mL) was diluted in 3.0mL of millipore water and placed in quartz cuvette with a pathlength of 1.0cm. The fluorescence measurement was recorded as initial intensity. A specific amount of Fe3+ solution (0.1M) was added to the CDs solution and the spectral measurements were recorded after each addition. The final concentrations of Fe3+ titrated were in the range of 0–32.7µM. A series of Fe3+ volume was micro-titrated to investigate the effective amount to fully quench the fluorescence. All the fluorescence data were recorded under fixed excitation of 350nm with both emission and excitation slits of 10nm.

To avoid interference from other possible cations, selectivity study was carried out by adding 50µL of other 0.1M cations (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+) in CDs sensing system to obtain a final concentration of 1.61mM. Fluorescence spectrum was recorded before any cations were added, which was taken as the blank signal. Another spectrum was recorded after the cation solution was added and the difference in fluorescence intensity was obtained as the sensing signal.

For the determination of AA, Fe3+ was added to the CDs sensing system and denoted as CDs-Fe3+. Different amount of AA (2.0mg/mL) was titrated to the CDs-Fe3+ system, amounting to final concentrations of AA ranging from 0 to 66µg/mL. The sensing mixture was left standing for 5min of incubation before the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at each addition of AA and the degree of fluorescence enhancement was calculated as the sensing signal for AA.

The CDs-Fe3+ sensing system was tested for its selectivity towards AA. A few amino acids, vitamins and possibly interfering compounds/small molecules were evaluated. Briefly, the optimised sensing system of 50µL CDs250 (2.0mg/mL) were quenched by 10µL Fe(NO3)3 (0.1M). Fluorescence of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system was recorded as the initial intensity before addition of the possible interfering species. 200µL of the interfering species was added into the CDs-Fe3+ solution and the fluorescence were then recorded again. The selectivity of the CDs-Fe3+ sensing system was also determined in the presence of possible interfering species such as glycine, glucose, glutamic acid, cysteine, thiamine and calcium chloride. The fluorescence was recorded after addition of AA in the presence of the interferences, same as the steps described above.

For real sample analysis, the AA content of vitamin C soluble tablet (Flavettes®) was evaluated using the CDs-Fe3+ system developed in this study. Briefly, a tablet (4.4967g) was weighed and crushed into powder form. The solution concentration of 25mg/mL was prepared upon analysis using CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence sensing system. For validation, the AA content of the tablet was compared to the existing titration method using DCPIP, as recommended by the Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC).

The CDs was obtained from a single step carbonisation method that used sodium alginate as the molecular precursor. Besides the chemical approach via acid dehydration previously carried out by our group (Fong et al., 2015), this pyrolytic carbonisation of carbohydrate source is another green and sustainable synthetic approach to obtain blue fluorescent CDs. This synthetic method used less chemical reagents while also produced minimal side products. Pyrolysis is a process in which physical decomposition and charring occurred due to high temperatures. Generally, this step has been adopted in the industrial sector to convert waste to carbon dioxide in the absence of oxygen for energy generation. It is still not well explored yet on the possibilities of simple pyrolysis step of simple organic precursor to obtain fluorescent CDs that could be used for many applications.

The pyrolysis was carried out in a furnace under the presence of atmospheric oxygen. Herein, to optimise the condition of heat carbonisation, starting materials were pyrolysed at different temperatures ranging between 150–350°C. The optimal condition was determined by monitoring the fluorescence intensity from samples prepared that showed the highest intensity with the same amount taken. As shown in Fig. S1, 150°C was not sufficient to carbonise sodium alginate and therefore the product collected was not fluorescent. The fluorescence intensity of CDs collected increased and reached an optimal temperature at 250°C and then slowly decreased as the higher temperature was adopted for the pyrolytic carbonisation process. Besides, the water solubility of CDs is another factor investigated for this synthesis. The CDs yielded at 300°C and above showed poor solubility in water. This could be due to a higher degree of charring at these temperatures. Charring refers to incomplete combustion that occurs during pyrolysis at high temperature. The charring effect can result in the formation of carbon residues that have a low content of oxygen and hydrogen. Therefore, the water solubility of the chars produced at higher temperature is significantly reduced. As a result, the reaction yield has decreased as more starting precursor was converted to char. This was observed for the CDs synthesised at 300°C and above. Subsequently, CDs obtained from 250°C was used for further sensing analysis, denoted as CDs250.

The sodium alginate precursor was broken down into smaller parts through physical and chemical decomposition via thermal carbonisation, common to a process of pyrolysis. The conventional pyrolysis is carried out in nitrogen flow, slowing down the heating process and thereby higher temperature required to achieve the charring effect. Differed from the conventional pyrolysis, the pyrolysis of sodium alginate, in this case, was performed in the presence of oxygen. Therefore, the temperature required was a lot lower than the conventional pyrolysis. Besides, oxidation occurred on the surface of the material amidst the chemical decomposition, which could further explain the change in the optical properties.

CDs250 was characterised for its morphology using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to the TEM image represented in Fig. 1(A), CDs250 obtained in this study is homogeneously dispersed and are mostly quasispherical nanoparticles. The size of CDs250 is in the range below the dimension of 10nm (Fig. 1 (B)) which is comparable to the CDs in the literature (Hu et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2015).

The CDs were further characterised with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. In order to understand the effect and progress of pyrolysis on CDs formation, samples collected at different carbonisation temperatures (ie. 200 and 250°C) after an hour were compared to the starting material and the IR observation is as shown in Fig. S2. The strong and broad vibrational stretching at 3300–3500cm−1 was attributed to the O-H group, where the peak has significantly become sharper after the pyrolysis (Hou et al., 2013). This could be due to the loss of water after the carbonisation. Coupled with the observation that C=O stretching (slight shifting from 1610 to 1573cm−1), of carboxylic acid indicated the changes in the chemical structure of alginate to CDs (Tan et al., 2013). This suggested the conversion of the carboxyl group of the starting material to the aldehyde or ketone in the final collected CDs. It has further strengthened the hypothesis that the oxidation has occurred on the surface during the pyrolytic carbonisation process. The glycosidic bond linking the monomers that made up sodium alginate has been broken during the dehydration process, which explains the absence of the symmetrical C-O stretch 1304cm−1. Furthermore, the fingerprint region between 500 and 1000cm−1 in CDs250 showed cleaner spectrum compared to that of alginate, indicating less functional groups present. As carbonisation is capable of decomposing functional groups, the observation of fingerprint region has proven that carbonisation did occur.

The optical properties of the obtained CDs are shown in Fig. 2. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, a small shoulder at about 270nm with a tail extending to longer wavelengths was observed. The absorbance peak at 270nm was mainly attributed to the π–π* energy transition of the CDs and this indicated the presence of conjugated system or C=C bonding (Wu et al., 2014). The fluorescence emission peaks were recorded at around 450nm upon excitation at 350nm, with a Stokes shift of 100nm. Upon excitation at a range of wavelengths from 280nm to 500nm, the optimal emission peak was determined at 450nm as shown in the contour plot in Fig. S3. The full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the as-prepared CDs was calculated as 105nm. According to the comparative method in which quinine sulphate in 0.1M H2SO4 was used as a fluorescence standard, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was found to be ~2%, which is reasonably common for the bare CDs. The mechanism of fluorescence for the CDs is still debatable, although mostly claimed to be attributed to the surface defects, recombinant and quantum effect (Shi et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2006). Similar to most of the reported CDs, the as-prepared CDs possess excitation-dependent fluorescence emission upon excitation within the UV region (Sun et al., 2006; Wang et al., 2011). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs did not fluctuate a lot throughout the basic and acidic pH range, enabling it to work in a wide range of pH. According to Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of CDs250 only showed a slight increase as the pH of the sample is higher. This could be explained by the surface chemistry due to the deprotonation of CDs oxidised surface with chemical functionality such as the –COOH group.

A comparison of fluorescence emission profile was made between the two CDs samples obtained via two different temperatures in a pyrolytic manner. Upon excitation at the optimised wavelength of 350nm, CDs200 repeatedly emitted at the peak of 435nm, whereas the optimal fluorescence emission of CDs250 was red shifted by 15nm–450nm. At the same concentration, CDs prepared at a higher temperature (ie. 250°C) emitted with higher intensity but lower PLQY. This indicates that different degree of carbonisation can take place at a different temperature. Higher temperature has resulted in more complete carbonisation and therefore causing the red shift of fluorescence. The bathochromic or red shift effect observed in CDs250 could be caused by the extension of conjugation (Meier et al., 2004) as a result of dehydrogenation that took place during the carbonisation process. Since sodium alginate was heated at high temperature in the presence of oxygen, carbonyl groups were still present as shown in the IR spectrum and the electron withdrawing effects by the carbonyl moieties could be enhanced. The stronger fluorescence of CDs250, when compared to the others in the same concentration, could be due to higher amount of the electron withdrawing group such as carbonyl group as a result of the carbonisation. This has shown that temperature can be a manipulative factor to control the emission of the CDs. The CDs emitting at longer wavelength could be useful for later development of sensor device that is more favourable towards longer wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditioning and probing of sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Most of the fluorescence detection systems are based on the “turn-off” model where the fluorescence of the fluorophore is quenched. In this system, we proposed and tested a “turn-on” sensing mechanism, where the fluorescence of the CDs needs to be quenched first and then restored using an analyte of interest. In order to achieve this, the conditioning and probing of the CDs-based sensing system were carried out. The blue emitting CDs were tested against a series of metal cations for the best quenching effect. Transition metal complexation is one of the main contributing factors that can affect the intrinsic near-ultraviolet fluorescence of many organic compounds (Waite and Morel, 1984). Species with unpaired electron spins are generally prone to significantly increase the singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing rate (Hercules, 1966; Waite and Morel, 1984). With that in mind, several paramagnetic transition metals ions such as Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Co2+ with partially filled d orbitals could potentially quench the fluorescence via intra-molecular energy transfer (Kniseley, 1969; Waite and Morel, 1984). Amongst the metal cations screened, Ca2+, Sn2+ and Pb2+ did affect the fluorescence of CDs, but rendered negligible as compared to the quenching effect in the presence of Fe3+ (refer to Fig. 3(A)). The excess amount of Fe3+ that serves as the quencher might overload the sensing system and result in inefficient recovery of fluorescence for the next step. Therefore, it is essential to find out the effective amount of Fe3+ needed to fully quench the fluorescence of CDs for an efficient sensing system while not in excess. For this purpose, a titration of Fe3+ to CDs sensing solution was carried out. The result has been plotted in Fig. 3(B) and (C). The degree of quenching has increased with the increasing amount of Fe3+ added and reached a plateau stage at approximately 0.30mM of Fe3+. Therefore, Fe3+ was added at a slightly higher final concentration of 0.32mM to ensure a complete turn off of the CDs fluorescence for the following detection of AA. In comparison with Gong's work, our system requires lower concentration of quencher, Fe3+ (0.30mM) to fully ‘turn-off’ the fluorescence (Gong et al., 2015). This could be of advantage in terms of sensor development as it could also reduce the overall production cost. Besides, it is also very important to note that the presence of Fe3+ in the sample could be a potential inhibitor of our ultimate goal, AA quantification.

A disruption of the interaction between the quencher cation and a fluorophore or a decrease in the ability of the quencher cation to enhance the rate of intersystem crossing in other ways such as a change in metal oxidation state will result in an increase in observed fluorescence (Waite and Morel, 1984). This is the rationale behind our “turn-on” model for AA detection. AA is stabilised by electron delocalization predominantly and present in the form of ascorbate anions. Due to its antioxidant and reducing properties, it could easily attack the positively charged Fe3+ cations and reduce the oxidation state of the cations from +3 to +2. As a result, the complexation between Fe3+ and CDs will be disrupted and restoration of CDs fluorescence can take place. This phenomenon could be exploited for the quantitative determination of AA. Micro-titration was performed to study the efficiency of this proposed sensing mechanism. In Fig. 4(A), it showed the initial blank CDs-Fe3+ solution that had low or no fluorescence but the fluorescence was recovered after addition of AA.

The interaction and fluorescence response in the presence of AA is observed by plotting the normalised intensity (F2/F1) against the concentration of AA as shown in Fig. 4(B), F2 being the intensity upon addition of AA and F1 is the initial intensity of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system before titration of AA. At a very low concentration of AA below 20µg/mL, the AA added into CDs-Fe3+ was not sufficient to overcome the binding between CDs and Fe3+. As a result, removal of Fe3+ was not effective. However, the enhancement of fluorescence started above that concentration and reached a plateau phase above 60µg/mL where saturation of species in the system occurred. In order to simplify the system, a dynamic range of AA was focused in the linear range, between 24 and 40µg/mL (refer to Fig. 4(B) inset). It is important to note that the presence of Fe3+ in samples could be a potential inhibitor for accurate AA quantification as it may interfere with the fluorescence ‘turn-off’ and leads to error in determining the actual amount of AA in the food sample. Despite this concern, Fe3+ is usually not present in high concentrations in potential food samples for our application. According to Fig. 3(C), fluorescence of CDs will not be affected significantly at very low concentrations of Fe3+ and the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched fully by Fe3+ at very low concentration (0.30mM). Therefore, the detection of AA should not be affected by the presence of Fe3+ in the food samples as long as the Fe3+ content is low or in negligible amount.

For an excellent sensing probe, the selectivity towards the target analyte without other interferences and possible false positive has to be ensured and evaluated. Therefore, selectivity against other possible interfering species including vitamins and amino acids of similar nature was investigated, as shown in Fig. 4(C). At a final concentration of 54µg/mL added, most of the tested amino acids did not bring about significant changes or recovery in the fluorescence intensity. The CDs-Fe3+ system was the most sensitive and selective towards AA, which allowed AA sensing to be carried out more accurately. Other than studying the effects of interferences on the fluorescence of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system, the selectivity towards AA of the sensor was also determined in the presence of possible interfering species, such as glycine, glucose, glutamic acid, cysteine, thiamine, and calcium chloride (Table S1). The presence of cysteine is found to affect the AA detection the most, as compared to the rest of tested interfering species. Despite this finding, our targeted real sample such as supplementary tablets and fruits or vegetables that have high content of AA most often have very little or none of cysteine present. Therefore, the selectivity for AA detection could still be valid for food science applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on CDs-Fe3+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the performance of the CDs-Fe3+ sensing system was investigated. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence changes for CDs in the presence of Fe3+ and CDs-Fe3+ in the presence of AA at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 5(A), the fluorescence quenching of the CDs was effective for pH ranged between 4.5 and 10 but less effective under acidic conditions below pH 4. The possible reason for this can be due to the surface charge of CDs. Under low pH conditions, the organic moieties such as carboxyl groups available richly on the surface of CDs will be protonated and results in the high positively charged surface (Bhattacharya et al., 2016). Such phenomena will lead to a repulsion of positively charged Fe3+ from CDs. Consequently, the fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ at low pH is ineffective due to reduced interaction between CDs and the quencher. As the pH increases, the protonation of carboxyl group on the surface of CDs is slowly taken over by deprotonation process, resulting in the negatively charged carboxyl groups that are able to attract the oppositely charged Fe3+ (Ngu et al., 2016; Tiong et al., 2015). This observation has therefore achieved the aim to fully ‘turn-off’ the fluorescence of CDs for AA sensing in the subsequent step. In this study, it was found out that the CDs system as it without prior pH control was having an average pH of 7.67±0.38 (n=9). This nicely falls within the optimal range to turn-off the fluorescence of CDs using Fe3+ and thus no further adjustment of pH is needed in this study. However, pH control can be introduced if necessary in future work when the pH range is out from this optimum range.

As for the restoration of the fluorescence in the presence of AA, the most effective pH range was found to be pH 4.5 and 8 (Fig. 5(B)). The pKa value of AA is around 4.25 and at higher pH value such as 7.4, the concentration of the mono-anionic form (AH−) is higher than that of the protonated form (AH2) (Giulivi and Cadenas, 1993). It is suggested that the dissociation of AA is required for its participation in electron transfer reactions. At pH value below the pKa, the dissociation of AA is limited and leads to lower availability of the active ionic AA to restore the fluorescence signal. The effective pH range for fluorescence restoration was also the range for the effective fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+. This gives a strong indication that the system was stable and can perform optimally in the pH range of 4.5–8, which covers physiological conditions and pH of most food consumed by human. Therefore, this finding suggests the great potential of our sensor for AA determination in food industry.

The feasibility of our nominated CDs-based fluorescence “turn-on” sensing for AA in the real sample has been tested. The sample selected was vitamin C soluble tablet of the brand, Flavette® that is commercially available to the public. The composition of each tablet includes 1000mg of vitamin C, 250mg of calcium and 300IU vitamin D. It was evaluated and validated using our system and the existing DCPIP titration method. Despite the fact that calcium and vitamin D are present in tablet, these two components did not interfere with the sensing. The selectivity analysis in Fig. 5(C) has shown that calcium did not bring about any significant changes to the fluorescence. The results were summarised in Table 1. Compared to the AA amount recorded using the well-established DCPIP method, the recovery rate of our system remained below 110% after three repetitions of the procedures. These results proved that this fluorescence “off-on” sensing system based on CDs-Fe3+ complexation was likely to be capable of real sample analysis.

Blue emitting fluorescent CDs have been successfully synthesised from sodium alginate via the sustainable one-step pyrolytic approach. The CDs have been found to be effectively quenched by Fe3+ due to the oxygen-rich surface. Optimisation has been carried out for the best condition of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system for our target analyte, AA. Being strong antioxidants with well-known reducing properties, AA is able to reduce Fe3+ of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system and restore the fluorescence which allows determination of AA. We have successfully demonstrated a unique fluorescence signalling strategy by the “turn-on” of CDs-Fe3+ sensing system that enabled highly specific detection of AA. Further work on developing or fabricating sensing probe based on this proposed system will be carried out.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by Swinburne Sarawak Research Grant phase 1/2013 (2-5509) from Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus. Jessica Fong would like to thank Swinburne SUPRA Scholarship for supporting her PhD study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.05.087.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
